column10: A rainbow happens when sunlight shines into tiny drops of water in the air, bounces around inside them, and comes out separated into all its colors: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and violet. Sunlight has all these colors inside all the time; to see them, we need the help of special shapes of clear liquids or solids like water, prisms, or crystals. The water needed to make rainbows has been on Earth for more than 4 billion years–almost as long as Earth has been a planet. For most of our history, humans did not know what made rainbows, so they thought they were magical or mysterious–and they made up many stories about them. About 2000 years ago in Greece, a curious learner named Aristotle described part of the science and reason why we see rainbows in nature. About 700 years ago in Iran, the scientist Kamāl al-Dīn al-Fārisī figured out and explained the real reason rainbows appear. You too might see a rainbow the next time the sun is behind you and rain is falling in front of you.
